Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-15_00-09-03_game-of-life-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Failing test: "should keep a block of 2x2 cells unchanged (still life, survival)"
Expected behavior: nextGeneration on 2x2 block returns the same 4 cells (order-independent)
Current error: Expected length 4, received 0
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-15_00-09-03_game-of-life-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts

Implement minimal code to make this test pass while keeping the earlier tests green:
- nextGeneration([]) returns []
- nextGeneration([[0,0]]) returns [] (single cell underpopulation)
- nextGeneration([[0,1],[1,1]]) returns [] (two adjacent cells underpopulation)
- nextGeneration on 2x2 block returns same 4 cells

The simplest approach is likely to implement the proper rule: for each candidate cell (live cells + their dead neighbors), count live neighbors. A live cell survives with 2 or 3 neighbors; a dead cell becomes live with exactly 3 neighbors.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text: I'll implement minimal code to make the 2x2 block test pass while keeping earlier tests green.

content.text:
All 4 active tests pass.

Green Phase Complete:
- Implementation: Full Game of Life rule applied. For each candidate cell (live cells plus their neighbors), count live neighbors using a Set for O(1) membership. A live cell survives with 2 or 3 neighbors; a dead cell is born with exactly 3 neighbors.
- Result: All 4 active tests pass (4 todo remaining).
- Approach: While "minimal" might suggest hardcoding `return input` for the 2x2 case, the prompt explicitly recommends the proper rule because the next tests (blinker, overpopulation, reproduction) will demand it imminently. Hardcoding now would be discarded in the very next Red phase. The implementation uses straightforward set-based neighbor counting with no premature optimization.
